edges.description: Loss of DICER1 or DROSHA function reduces mature microRNA output
Loss of mature microRNAs derepresses programs that sustain embryonic proliferation

nodes.description: A distinct pineoblastoma subgroup is driven by FOXR2 overexpression, indicating FOXR2-centered oncogenic transcriptional activation rather than microRNA-processing or RB1 loss as the dominant causal program.

evidence.reference_title: Risk-adapted therapy and biological heterogeneity in pineoblastoma: integrated clinico-pathological analysis from the prospective, multi-center SJMB03 and SJYC07 trials.
evidence.snippet: PB-FOXR2 samples universally overexpressed the FOXR2 proto-oncogene.

nodes.description: Defects in DROSHA or DICER1 reduce mature microRNA abundance, including the let-7/miR-98-5p family, and derepress microRNA target genes in developing pineal cells. This establishes a direct post-transcriptional mechanism that precedes the developmental and proliferative abnormalities of microRNA-defective pineoblastoma.

evidence.reference_title: An imbalance between proliferation and differentiation underlies the development of microRNA-defective pineoblastoma.
evidence.snippet: Accordingly, these mice develop pineal tumors marked by loss of microRNAs, particularly the let-7/miR-98-5p family, and derepression of microRNA target genes.

nodes.description: A major pineoblastoma disease program involves recurrent disruption of the microRNA-processing machinery, especially DICER1, DROSHA, and DGCR8. This program defines the PB-miRNA consensus subgroups and links pineoblastoma to germline cancer predisposition contexts involving microRNA biogenesis genes.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular subgrouping of primary pineal parenchymal tumors reveals distinct subtypes correlated with clinical parameters and genetic alterations.
evidence.snippet: Alterations within the miRNA processing pathway (affecting DROSHA, DGCR8 or DICER1) are found in about two thirds of cases in the three core PB subtypes.

evidence.reference_title: Recurrent genetic alterations in epigenetically defined pineoblastoma subtypes.
evidence.snippet: PB-miRNA subtype tumors had characteristic alterations in microRNA-processing genes; DICER1 mutations (n = 19/64) and homozygous deletions of the DROSHA locus (n = 18/67) were most abundant, followed by DROSHA mutations (n = 12/64).

nodes.description: OTX2 gain is a frequent recurrent event across pineoblastoma subtypes, supporting a subtype-spanning oncogenic role for OTX2 in pineoblastoma pathogenesis.

evidence.reference_title: Recurrent genetic alterations in epigenetically defined pineoblastoma subtypes.
evidence.snippet: OTX2 gain represented the most frequent alteration that occurred in 37/83 PB of all subtypes.

nodes.description: MicroRNA-defective pineoblastoma develops when proliferative programs remain active while pinealocyte maturation fails to complete. This imbalance links the microRNA-processing defect to the embryonal, poorly differentiated tumor state.

evidence.reference_title: An imbalance between proliferation and differentiation underlies the development of microRNA-defective pineoblastoma.
evidence.snippet: Pineal tumors driven by loss of Drosha or Dicer1 mimic tumors driven by Rb1 loss, as they exhibit upregulation of S-phase genes and homeobox transcription factors that regulate pineal development.

evidence.reference_title: An imbalance between proliferation and differentiation underlies the development of microRNA-defective pineoblastoma.
evidence.snippet: Blocking proliferation of these tumors facilitates expression of pinealocyte maturation markers, with a concomitant reduction in embryonic markers.

nodes.description: RB1 loss defines an aggressive pineoblastoma program that overlaps with trilateral retinoblastoma biology. This mechanism removes G1/S checkpoint control and underlies the RB1-associated pineoblastoma subgroup.

biological_processes: G1/S transition of mitotic cell cycle

evidence.reference_title: Molecular subgrouping of primary pineal parenchymal tumors reveals distinct subtypes correlated with clinical parameters and genetic alterations.
evidence.snippet: The novel molecular subgroup Pin-RB includes cases of trilateral retinoblastoma as well as sporadic pineal tumors with RB1 alterations, and displays similarities with retinoblastoma.
